message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>The emergence of the highly transmissible SARS-CoV-2 Delta variant has created a need to reassess the risk posed by increasing social contacts as countries resume pre-pandemic activities, particularly in the context of resuming large-scale events over multiple days. To examine how social contacts formed in different activity settings influences interventions required to control outbreaks, we combined high-resolution data on contacts among passengers and crew on cruise ships with network transmission models. We found passengers had a median of 20 (IQR 10–36) unique close contacts per day, and over 60% of their contact episodes were made in dining or sports areas where mask wearing is typically limited. In simulated outbreaks, we found that vaccination coverage and rapid antigen tests had a larger effect than mask mandates alone, indicating the importance of combined interventions against Delta to reduce event risk in the vaccine era.</jats:p>
title: Using high-resolution contact networks to evaluate SARS-CoV-2 transmission and control in large-scale multi-day events
